1	---
2	created: 2026-04-05
3	updated: 2026-04-05
4	tags: [motion, gsap, css, easing, animation, reference]
5	source: reverse-engineering of jitter.video and shop.app/minis
6	---
7	
8	# Premium Motion Patterns
9	
10	Learnings from reverse-engineering two high-fidelity production sites: **Jitter** (jitter.video) and **Shopify Shop Minis** (shop.app/minis). Both represent top-tier web motion design — the kind of work that separates "animated" from "crafted."
11	
12	---
13	
14	## 1. Custom Easing Is Everything
15	
16	Neither site relies on CSS defaults. Every transition uses purpose-built curves.
17	
18	### Named Easings (from Shop Minis GSAP bundle)
19	
20	```js
21	CustomEase.create("snappy",     "M0,0 C0.19,1 0.22,1 1,1");
22	CustomEase.create("anticipate", "M0,0 C1,-0.4 0.35,0.95 1,1");
23	CustomEase.create("overshoot",  "M0,0 C0.175,0.885 0.32,1.275 1,1");
24	CustomEase.create("circ.inOut.soft",
25	  "M0,0 C0.17,0 0.286,0.085 0.32,0.115 0.394,0.18 0.478,0.301 0.5,0.5 0.522,0.706 0.608,0.816 0.645,0.852 0.67,0.877 0.794,1 1,1");
26	```
27	
28	**When to use each:**
29	- **snappy** — fast initial burst, smooth land. Good for elements entering the viewport or snapping into place.
30	- **anticipate** — pulls *backward* first (y1=-0.4), then shoots forward. Creates a "winding up" feel. Used for carousel snap and dramatic entrances.
31	- **overshoot** — goes past target (y2=1.275), then settles. Subtle spring feel without actual spring physics. Good for buttons, tags, small UI elements.
32	- **circ.inOut.soft** — a softened circular ease. Symmetric, smooth, no harsh edges. Used for card stack transitions where you want elegance, not energy.
33	
34	### Jitter's Easing Palette
35	
36	```css
37	cubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1)       /* expo-out — smooth deceleration */
38	cubic-bezier(0.22, 0.73, 0.3, 1.39)  /* overshoot — hero media entrance */
39	cubic-bezier(0.33, 1.42, 0.05, 0.96) /* strong overshoot — tags, buttons */
40	cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94) /* smooth decel — hover transitions */
41	```
42	
43	### Key Insight
44	
45	Premium sites build an **easing vocabulary** — 4-6 named curves that get reused consistently. They don't pick random cubic-beziers per element. The curves become part of the brand's motion identity.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## 2. Spring Physics > Spring Easings
50	
51	Shop Minis uses a **real spring simulation** for its 3D carousel, not a CSS or GSAP easing:
52	
53	```js
54	function updateSpring() {
55	  const springForce = -stiffness * (current - target);
56	  const dampingForce = -damping * velocity;
57	  const acceleration = (springForce + dampingForce) / mass;
58	  velocity += acceleration;
59	  current += velocity;
60	}
61	```
62	
63	This runs on `gsap.ticker` (every frame). The values are responsive:
64	
65	| | Desktop (lg) | Tablet (md) | Mobile |
66	|---|---|---|---|
67	| stiffness | 0.025 | 0.05 | 0.05 |
68	| damping | 0.2 | 0.5 | 0.5 |
69	| mass | 1 | 0.5 | 0.5 |
70	
71	**Why spring physics over spring easing:**
72	- Natural overshoot and settle — no two snaps feel identical
73	- Velocity-aware — a fast flick overshoots more than a slow drag
74	- Interruptible — drag mid-spring and it responds immediately
75	- The "feel" is alive, not canned
76	
77	**When to use it:** Any drag-to-snap interaction where the user controls velocity. Carousels, sliders, pull-to-refresh, card decks.
78	
79	**When a spring easing is enough:** Non-interactive entrances where you just want bounce. GSAP's `elastic.out(1, 0.5)` works fine there.
80	
81	---
82	
83	## 3. Layered Intro Sequences
84	
85	Both sites orchestrate multi-timeline intros. The pattern:
86	
87	### Shop Minis Intro (3 parallel timelines)
88	
89	**Timeline 1 — Text**
90	```
91	t=0.00  Title scales in (0.5→1), elastic.out(1, 0.5), 2s
92	t=0.50  Subtitle slides up, circ.in, 0.25s
93	t=0.75  Container springs to center, elastic.out(0.9, 0.7), 1.25s
94	t=1.64  Paragraph + CTA fade in with stagger 70ms, snappy, 0.6s
95	```
96	
97	**Timeline 2 — Carousel**
98	```
99	t=0.00  Carousel slides up from scrollY, snappy, 0.625s
100	t=0.00  Cylinder rotates -180°→0° (half turn), anticipate, 1.5625s
101	t=0.80  Carousel fades in, none, 0.4s
102	t=0.94  Carousel settles to y:0, anticipate, 1.25s
103	t=3.44  Shadow gradient fades out
104	```
105	
106	**Timeline 3 — Per-card (6 cards, staggered backwards)**
107	```
108	Card 5: t=0.05  yPercent 200→0 + rotateY half→full, anticipate, 1.25s
109	Card 4: t=0.10  same
110	Card 3: t=0.15  same
111	Card 2: t=0.20  same
112	Card 1: t=0.25  same
113	Card 0: t=0.30  same
114	```
115	
116	### Jitter Intro
117	
118	```
119	Tag wrapper:    scale 0.75→1, 800ms, delay 450ms, strong overshoot
120	Button wrapper: scale 0→1, 800ms, delay 200ms, strong overshoot
121	Hero media:     scale 0→1, 550ms, delay 300ms, overshoot
122	Header bar:     translateY -100%→0, 1200ms, expo-out
123	```
124	
125	### The Pattern
126	
127	1. **Stagger backwards** — last elements animate first, creating a "fan out" effect
128	2. **Different easings per layer** — text gets elastic, containers get anticipate, UI elements get overshoot
129	3. **Overlap aggressively** — elements don't wait for each other. 50-200ms offsets, not sequential
130	4. **Longest animation sets the tone** — usually the main container or hero element (1.25-2s)
131	5. **Quick fades, slow transforms** — opacity changes are instant (0.1-0.4s), transforms are drawn out (1-2s)
132	
133	---
134	
135	## 4. 3D Carousel Construction
136	
137	### Cylinder Geometry
138	
139	```
140	6 cards × 60° apart = full cylinder
141	Radius: 28rem (mobile) / 50rem (tablet) / 60rem (desktop)
142	Perspective: 1800px
143	
144	Each card:
145	  transform: rotateY(var(--rotate)) translateZ(var(--translate));
146	  --rotate: 0deg / 60deg / 120deg / 180deg / 240deg / 300deg
147	  --translate: 50rem (= radius)
148	
149	Cylinder container:
150	  transform: translateZ(-50rem) rotateY(currentAngle);
151	  // translateZ pulls the cylinder back so the front card sits at z=0
152	```
153	
154	### Backface Strategy
155	
156	```css
157	/* Both faces use backface-visibility: hidden */
158	[data-card-back]  { transform: rotateY(-180deg) translateZ(1px); opacity: 0.99; }
159	[data-card-front] { transform: rotateY(0deg) translateZ(2px); opacity: 0.99; }
160	```
161	
162	- `translateZ(1px)` vs `2px` prevents z-fighting
163	- `opacity: 0.99` forces GPU compositing without visual difference
164	- Back face uses a shared dark texture with overlay — doesn't need unique content
165	
166	### Auto-Rotation
167	
168	```js
169	setInterval(() => {
170	  const next = (current + 1) % 6;
171	  spring.target = -(next * 60);  // negative because CSS rotateY is clockwise
172	}, 3000);
173	// Stops permanently on first user interaction
174	```
175	
176	---
177	
178	## 5. Drag Interaction Patterns
179	
180	### Velocity-Based Drag (Shop Minis)
181	
182	```js
183	onPointerMove(e) {
184	  const delta = e.clientX - lastX;
185	  lastX = e.clientX;
186	  spring.progress += delta / window.innerWidth;  // normalize to viewport
187	  spring.target = spring.progress * 360;
188	}
189	
190	onPointerUp() {
191	  // Snap to nearest card
192	  const nearest = Math.round(spring.progress * cardCount);
193	  spring.target = nearest * anglePerCard;
194	}
195	```
196	
197	Key details:
198	- Delta is **normalized to viewport width** — drag feels consistent across screen sizes
199	- Snap uses `Math.round` — spring physics handles the actual animation
200	- `cursor: grab` / `cursor: grabbing` + `touch-action: pan-y` for touch
201	
202	### Marquee Drag (Jitter)
203	
204	```css
205	.slider-marquee { cursor: grab; user-select: none; }
206	.slider-marquee:active { cursor: grabbing; }
207	.slider-scroller { display: flex; will-change: transform; }
208	```
209	
210	- JS-driven `translateX()` on the scroller
211	- Cards are duplicated (14 → 28) for seamless infinite loop
212	- `pointer-events: none` on children during drag prevents accidental clicks
213	
214	---
215	
216	## 6. Scroll-Driven Color Transitions
217	
218	Jitter uses per-word color transitions as the user scrolls:
219	
220	```
221	Scroll in:  rgb(151, 151, 155) → rgb(25, 23, 28)   (gray → near-black, 500ms)
222	Scroll out: rgb(52, 50, 55)    → rgb(129, 128, 132) (dark → muted, 500ms)
223	```
224	
225	Each word is an inline-block `div` — scroll position determines which words are "active." This creates a progressive text reveal that feels alive.
226	
227	---
228	
229	## 7. Sticky Card Stack Pattern (Jitter)
230	
231	Jitter's "No learning curve" section uses CSS sticky positioning for stacking cards:
232	
233	```css
234	/* Each card stacks below the previous with increasing top offset */
235	card 1: { top: 301.5px; margin-bottom: 50px; position: sticky; }
236	card 2: { top: 326.5px; margin-bottom: 25px; position: sticky; }
237	card 3: { top: 351.5px; margin-bottom: 0px;  position: sticky; }
238	```
239	
240	The decreasing `margin-bottom` (50→25→0) and increasing `top` (301→326→351) creates the stacking effect as you scroll. Pure CSS, no JS needed.
241	
242	---
243	
244	## 8. Recurring Patterns & Principles
245	
246	### Duration Ranges
247	- **Micro-interactions** (hover, toggle): 200-400ms
248	- **Element entrances**: 500-800ms
249	- **Hero/container animations**: 1-2s
250	- **Spring settle time**: 1-3s (depends on damping)
251	- **Auto-rotation interval**: 3000ms
252	
253	### The "Anticipate" Pattern
254	Pull back before pushing forward. Shows up everywhere in premium motion:
255	- Carousel snap pulls back ~15% before landing
256	- Button hover scales to 1.1 then settles to 1.0
257	- Card dismissal uses negative y1 in cubic-bezier
258	
259	### Responsive Motion
260	- Stiffer springs on mobile (less overshoot, quicker settle)
261	- Smaller radii/distances on mobile
262	- Same easings across breakpoints — timing changes, character doesn't
263	
264	### Invisible Starts
265	Both sites use `visibility: hidden` / `invisible` on elements, then animate in with `autoAlpha`. This prevents flash-of-unstyled-content while keeping elements in the DOM for layout.
266	
267	### GPU Hints
268	- `will-change: transform` on anything that moves
269	- `transform-style: preserve-3d` for 3D contexts
270	- `opacity: 0.99` to force compositing layers
271	- `-webkit-backface-visibility: hidden` always paired with standard property
272	
273	---
274	
275	## 9. Easing Quick Reference
276	
277	For copy-paste into projects:
278	
279	```css
280	:root {
281	  --ease-snappy: cubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1);
282	  --ease-overshoot: cubic-bezier(0.175, 0.885, 0.32, 1.275);
283	  --ease-overshoot-strong: cubic-bezier(0.33, 1.42, 0.05, 0.96);
284	  --ease-smooth-decel: cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94);
285	  --ease-expo-out: cubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1);
286	}
287	```
288	
289	```js

updated: 2026-04-05
tags: [motion, gsap, css, easing, animation, reference]
source: reverse-engineering of jitter.video and shop.app/minis
---

# Premium Motion Patterns

Learnings from reverse-engineering two high-fidelity production sites: **Jitter** (jitter.video) and **Shopify Shop Minis** (shop.app/minis). Both represent top-tier web motion design — the kind of work that separates "animated" from "crafted."

---

## 1. Custom Easing Is Everything

Neither site relies on CSS defaults. Every transition uses purpose-built curves.

### Named Easings (from Shop Minis GSAP bundle)

```js
CustomEase.create("snappy",     "M0,0 C0.19,1 0.22,1 1,1");
CustomEase.create("anticipate", "M0,0 C1,-0.4 0.35,0.95 1,1");
CustomEase.create("overshoot",  "M0,0 C0.175,0.885 0.32,1.275 1,1");
CustomEase.create("circ.inOut.soft",
  "M0,0 C0.17,0 0.286,0.085 0.32,0.115 0.394,0.18 0.478,0.301 0.5,0.5 0.522,0.706 0.608,0.816 0.645,0.852 0.67,0.877 0.794,1 1,1");
```

**When to use each:**
- **snappy** — fast initial burst, smooth land. Good for elements entering the viewport or snapping into place.
- **anticipate** — pulls *backward* first (y1=-0.4), then shoots forward. Creates a "winding up" feel. Used for carousel snap and dramatic entrances.
- **overshoot** — goes past target (y2=1.275), then settles. Subtle spring feel without actual spring physics. Good for buttons, tags, small UI elements.
- **circ.inOut.soft** — a softened circular ease. Symmetric, smooth, no harsh edges. Used for card stack transitions where you want elegance, not energy.

### Jitter's Easing Palette

```css
cubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1)       /* expo-out — smooth deceleration */
cubic-bezier(0.22, 0.73, 0.3, 1.39)  /* overshoot — hero media entrance */
cubic-bezier(0.33, 1.42, 0.05, 0.96) /* strong overshoot — tags, buttons */
cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94) /* smooth decel — hover transitions */
```

### Key Insight

Premium sites build an **easing vocabulary** — 4-6 named curves that get reused consistently. They don't pick random cubic-beziers per element. The curves become part of the brand's motion identity.

---

## 2. Spring Physics > Spring Easings

Shop Minis uses a **real spring simulation** for its 3D carousel, not a CSS or GSAP easing:

```js
function updateSpring() {
  const springForce = -stiffness * (current - target);
  const dampingForce = -damping * velocity;
  const acceleration = (springForce + dampingForce) / mass;
  velocity += acceleration;
  current += velocity;
}
```

This runs on `gsap.ticker` (every frame). The values are responsive:

| | Desktop (lg) | Tablet (md) | Mobile |
|---|---|---|---|
| stiffness | 0.025 | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| damping | 0.2 | 0.5 | 0.5 |
| mass | 1 | 0.5 | 0.5 |

**Why spring physics over spring easing:**
- Natural overshoot and settle — no two snaps feel identical
- Velocity-aware — a fast flick overshoots more than a slow drag
- Interruptible — drag mid-spring and it responds immediately
- The "feel" is alive, not canned

**When to use it:** Any drag-to-snap interaction where the user controls velocity. Carousels, sliders, pull-to-refresh, card decks.

**When a spring easing is enough:** Non-interactive entrances where you just want bounce. GSAP's `elastic.out(1, 0.5)` works fine there.

---

## 3. Layered Intro Sequences

Both sites orchestrate multi-timeline intros. The pattern:

### Shop Minis Intro (3 parallel timelines)

**Timeline 1 — Text**
```
t=0.00  Title scales in (0.5→1), elastic.out(1, 0.5), 2s
t=0.50  Subtitle slides up, circ.in, 0.25s
t=0.75  Container springs to center, elastic.out(0.9, 0.7), 1.25s
t=1.64  Paragraph + CTA fade in with stagger 70ms, snappy, 0.6s
```

**Timeline 2 — Carousel**
```
t=0.00  Carousel slides up from scrollY, snappy, 0.625s
t=0.00  Cylinder rotates -180°→0° (half turn), anticipate, 1.5625s
t=0.80  Carousel fades in, none, 0.4s
t=0.94  Carousel settles to y:0, anticipate, 1.25s
t=3.44  Shadow gradient fades out
```

**Timeline 3 — Per-card (6 cards, staggered backwards)**
```
Card 5: t=0.05  yPercent 200→0 + rotateY half→full, anticipate, 1.25s
Card 4: t=0.10  same
Card 3: t=0.15  same
Card 2: t=0.20  same
Card 1: t=0.25  same
Card 0: t=0.30  same
```

### Jitter Intro

```
Tag wrapper:    scale 0.75→1, 800ms, delay 450ms, strong overshoot
Button wrapper: scale 0→1, 800ms, delay 200ms, strong overshoot
Hero media:     scale 0→1, 550ms, delay 300ms, overshoot
Header bar:     translateY -100%→0, 1200ms, expo-out
```

### The Pattern

1. **Stagger backwards** — last elements animate first, creating a "fan out" effect
2. **Different easings per layer** — text gets elastic, containers get anticipate, UI elements get overshoot
3. **Overlap aggressively** — elements don't wait for each other. 50-200ms offsets, not sequential
4. **Longest animation sets the tone** — usually the main container or hero element (1.25-2s)
5. **Quick fades, slow transforms** — opacity changes are instant (0.1-0.4s), transforms are drawn out (1-2s)

---

## 4. 3D Carousel Construction

### Cylinder Geometry

```
6 cards × 60° apart = full cylinder
Radius: 28rem (mobile) / 50rem (tablet) / 60rem (desktop)
Perspective: 1800px

Each card:
  transform: rotateY(var(--rotate)) translateZ(var(--translate));
  --rotate: 0deg / 60deg / 120deg / 180deg / 240deg / 300deg
  --translate: 50rem (= radius)

Cylinder container:
  transform: translateZ(-50rem) rotateY(currentAngle);
  // translateZ pulls the cylinder back so the front card sits at z=0
```

### Backface Strategy

```css
/* Both faces use backface-visibility: hidden */
[data-card-back]  { transform: rotateY(-180deg) translateZ(1px); opacity: 0.99; }
[data-card-front] { transform: rotateY(0deg) translateZ(2px); opacity: 0.99; }
```

- `translateZ(1px)` vs `2px` prevents z-fighting
- `opacity: 0.99` forces GPU compositing without visual difference
- Back face uses a shared dark texture with overlay — doesn't need unique content

### Auto-Rotation

```js
setInterval(() => {
  const next = (current + 1) % 6;
  spring.target = -(next * 60);  // negative because CSS rotateY is clockwise
}, 3000);
// Stops permanently on first user interaction
```

---

## 5. Drag Interaction Patterns

### Velocity-Based Drag (Shop Minis)

```js
onPointerMove(e) {
  const delta = e.clientX - lastX;
  lastX = e.clientX;
  spring.progress += delta / window.innerWidth;  // normalize to viewport
  spring.target = spring.progress * 360;
}

onPointerUp() {
  // Snap to nearest card
  const nearest = Math.round(spring.progress * cardCount);
  spring.target = nearest * anglePerCard;
}
```

Key details:
- Delta is **normalized to viewport width** — drag feels consistent across screen sizes
- Snap uses `Math.round` — spring physics handles the actual animation
- `cursor: grab` / `cursor: grabbing` + `touch-action: pan-y` for touch

### Marquee Drag (Jitter)

```css
.slider-marquee { cursor: grab; user-select: none; }
.slider-marquee:active { cursor: grabbing; }
.slider-scroller { display: flex; will-change: transform; }
```

- JS-driven `translateX()` on the scroller
- Cards are duplicated (14 → 28) for seamless infinite loop
- `pointer-events: none` on children during drag prevents accidental clicks

---

## 6. Scroll-Driven Color Transitions

Jitter uses per-word color transitions as the user scrolls:

```
Scroll in:  rgb(151, 151, 155) → rgb(25, 23, 28)   (gray → near-black, 500ms)
Scroll out: rgb(52, 50, 55)    → rgb(129, 128, 132) (dark → muted, 500ms)
```

Each word is an inline-block `div` — scroll position determines which words are "active." This creates a progressive text reveal that feels alive.

---

## 7. Sticky Card Stack Pattern (Jitter)

Jitter's "No learning curve" section uses CSS sticky positioning for stacking cards:

```css
/* Each card stacks below the previous with increasing top offset */
card 1: { top: 301.5px; margin-bottom: 50px; position: sticky; }
card 2: { top: 326.5px; margin-bottom: 25px; position: sticky; }
card 3: { top: 351.5px; margin-bottom: 0px;  position: sticky; }
```

The decreasing `margin-bottom` (50→25→0) and increasing `top` (301→326→351) creates the stacking effect as you scroll. Pure CSS, no JS needed.

---

## 8. Recurring Patterns & Principles

### Duration Ranges
- **Micro-interactions** (hover, toggle): 200-400ms
- **Element entrances**: 500-800ms
- **Hero/container animations**: 1-2s
- **Spring settle time**: 1-3s (depends on damping)
- **Auto-rotation interval**: 3000ms

### The "Anticipate" Pattern
Pull back before pushing forward. Shows up everywhere in premium motion:
- Carousel snap pulls back ~15% before landing
- Button hover scales to 1.1 then settles to 1.0
- Card dismissal uses negative y1 in cubic-bezier

### Responsive Motion
- Stiffer springs on mobile (less overshoot, quicker settle)
- Smaller radii/distances on mobile
- Same easings across breakpoints — timing changes, character doesn't

### Invisible Starts
Both sites use `visibility: hidden` / `invisible` on elements, then animate in with `autoAlpha`. This prevents flash-of-unstyled-content while keeping elements in the DOM for layout.

### GPU Hints
- `will-change: transform` on anything that moves
- `transform-style: preserve-3d` for 3D contexts
- `opacity: 0.99` to force compositing layers
- `-webkit-backface-visibility: hidden` always paired with standard property

---

## 9. Easing Quick Reference

For copy-paste into projects:

```css
:root {
  --ease-snappy: cubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1);
  --ease-overshoot: cubic-bezier(0.175, 0.885, 0.32, 1.275);
  --ease-overshoot-strong: cubic-bezier(0.33, 1.42, 0.05, 0.96);
  --ease-smooth-decel: cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94);
  --ease-expo-out: cubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1);
}
```

```js
// GSAP CustomEase equivalents
CustomEase.create("snappy",     "M0,0 C0.19,1 0.22,1 1,1");
CustomEase.create("anticipate", "M0,0 C1,-0.4 0.35,0.95 1,1");
CustomEase.create("overshoot",  "M0,0 C0.175,0.885 0.32,1.275 1,1");
```

---

## Related Notes

- [[swiper-gsap-patterns]] — GSAP + Swiper integration patterns
- [[Motion best practices]] — general motion principles (Locomotive talk)
- [[gsap-deterministic-loops]] — deterministic timeline loops for video export
- [[webgl-tooling-patterns]] — WebGL tooling and export pipeline
- [[step-sequencer-animation]] — sequencer-based orchestration (alternative to chained timelines)

# Motion Design Principles

How motion should work on the web. These are the rules — follow them when building any animation, transition, or interaction.

Source: Motion best practices course (Locomotive/Pangram Pangram designer). See also [[storytelling-in-webdesign]] for how animation supports narrative.

---

## Where to use motion

| Location | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Preloader** | Make the wait enjoyable, set personality | Pangram Pangram rotating intro |
| **Page transitions** | Eliminate dead time between pages, preserve visual continuity | Elements that exist on both pages animate to new position |
| **Entry animations** | First impression — guide attention to key elements | Hero CTA flashes at end of load |
| **Hover / micro-interactions** | Show that an action is available, add personality | Button reveals what will happen on click |
| **Cursor-driven** | Create depth and perspective | Parallax on mouse position |
| **Scroll-triggered** | Reveal elements as user scrolls, add dimension | Parallax, fade-in, pin sections |
| **Continuous / ambient** | Small looping animations that tell the story | Background elements, subtle movement |
| **Feedback / confirmation** | Show result of user action | Vote animation, add-to-cart confirmation |

---

## Timing

The most important skill. Same animation at different speeds feels completely different.

- **Too slow:** user has to wait — feels broken
- **Too fast:** steps blend together — changes are invisible
- **Just 15% off** in either direction is enough to ruin it

### Duration guidelines

| Scope | Duration | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Micro-interaction (button, icon) | ~0.3–0.5s | Small area = can be quick |
| Element entrance | ~0.4–0.8s | Depends on distance traveled |
| Full-screen transition | ~0.8–1.2s | Bigger area needs more time |
| Complex multi-step animation | 1.0–2.0s+ | Give each step room to breathe |

These are starting points — always adjust by feel. The right duration depends on complexity and area covered, not on a fixed number.

---

## Easing

Speed should never be constant — that looks mechanical. Elements accelerate and decelerate like real objects.

| Easing | Motion | Use for |
|---|---|---|
| **Ease out** | Starts fast, slows at end | Elements entering the screen |
| **Ease in** | Starts slow, ends fast | Elements leaving the screen |
| **Ease in-out** | Accelerates then decelerates | Elements moving but staying on screen |
| **Linear (no easing)** | Constant speed | Continuous motion: clouds, tickers, progress bars |

### Recommended CSS/GSAP values

```css
/* Standard ease out — good default for entrances */
transition-timing-function: cubic-bezier(0.25, 0, 0.15, 1);

/* Snappy ease out — for UI interactions */
transition-timing-function: cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1);

/* Smooth ease in-out — for position changes */
transition-timing-function: cubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1);
```

```js
// GSAP equivalents
gsap.to(el, { duration: 0.6, ease: "power2.out" });   // standard
gsap.to(el, { duration: 0.4, ease: "power3.out" });   // snappy
gsap.to(el, { duration: 0.8, ease: "power2.inOut" }); // smooth

Nå er det skikkelig fikset. Thumbnails bruker nå `<canvas>` istedenfor `<video>`:

input.content:
Widforss Shorts

Feature specification for a vertical video feed on widforss.se — v1, June 2025
Prototype: index.html in this folder · Production plan: PLAN.md

What this is

A short-form vertical video feed, similar to what you'd find on TikTok or Instagram Reels, but owned by Widforss and embedded on the site. The idea is to strengthen social proof and let our community (and eventually customers) share outdoor content directly tied to products we sell.

The prototype in this folder covers the core UX and interaction patterns for both desktop and mobile. It's meant as a starting point — not everything is pixel-final, and there's room to adjust as we build it out together.

Desktop layout

Video player

  • The video sits centered in the content area, below the standard site header/navigation.
  • Video card size: 660 × 825px, border-radius 12px.
  • Background behind the video: #1D1D1D (dark, full-width content area).
  • A subtle bottom gradient overlays the lower portion of the video to make the progress bar readable.

Scroll behavior

  • Vertical scroll with snap-to-center — each scroll gesture locks to the next or previous video.
  • Mouse wheel, keyboard (arrow keys), and click navigation all work.
  • When a clip finishes playing, it automatically advances to the next one.
  • The feed is infinite — when the user reaches the end of the available clips, we shuffle and append more. No clip should appear twice in a row.

Left side — info panel

  • Vertically centered to the left of the video.
  • Shows: title (24px, semibold) and timestamp (16px, light, e.g. "10 min siden").
  • When a video has tagged products, a product thumbnail bubble appears below the title. The bubble has a dark container (135 × 138px, radius 23px, #404040 background) with the product image inside (radius 20px). Some products have a white background instead — this should come from CMS data.
  • Below the bubble: a label like "Used: Crispi Summit GTX" and a "Shop here" button (#AFCC95 pill).
  • Videos without products don't show the bubble or button — just title and timestamp.

Right side — thumbnail navigation

  • Three video thumbnails stacked vertically, showing previous/current/next clips.
  • The active (center) thumbnail is slightly larger than the others — there's a smooth scale transition when scrolling between videos. The inactive ones sit at roughly 82% scale, the active at 100%.
  • Up/down chevron buttons above and below the thumbnails.
  • Clicking a thumbnail jumps to that video.

Progress bar

  • Sits at the bottom of the video card.
  • Dark track (rgba(29, 27, 25, 0.9)), green fill (#7EB896), both rounded.
  • Clickable — the user can click anywhere on the bar to skip to that point in the video.
  • Hit target is larger than the visual bar (20px tall, bar itself is 4px) for easy clicking.

Shop the Look drawer (desktop)

  • Clicking "Shop here" opens a white drawer sliding in from the right (420px wide).
  • Shows all tagged products for that video: image, brand, name, price, and an "Add to cart" button per product.
  • The video pauses while the drawer is open.
  • Closes via the X button, clicking the overlay behind it, or scrolling to a different video.

Mute toggle

  • Bottom-right of the content area.
  • Videos start muted. Click or press M to toggle.

Mobile layout

On mobile the experience shifts to a full-bleed TikTok-style layout:

  • The standard site header is replaced by a compact mobile header: hamburger menu | logo (108px) | cart icon. Below that, a search field.
  • The video fills the entire remaining viewport — no border radius, no side panels.
  • The thumbnail sidebar is hidden. Navigation is swipe-only (vertical scroll snap).
  • Title, timestamp, and a "Se produktene" button overlay the bottom of the video, over a darkened gradient.
  • The product bubble is not shown on mobile (too much visual noise at this size). Instead, the CTA opens a dark drawer from the right (240px, #2a2a2a) with product cards — image, brand, name, price, and add-to-cart.
  • The video pauses while the product drawer is open.
  • The progress bar and mute toggle are repositioned for thumb reach.
Note: The prototype includes a Desktop/Mobile toggle in the top-right corner so you can preview both layouts in one page.

Product tagging not final

This is an area we'd like to explore further — the visual design isn't fully locked down yet, but the concept is important to get into the build early.

The basic idea: each video can have one or more products associated with it. These come from the product catalog (Centra) and are linked in the CMS (Sanity).

Some directions we're thinking about:

  • Timed product tags — a product pill could appear on the video at a specific timestamp (e.g. when the creator holds up a jacket at second 8, the tag pops up and disappears at second 14). This would need start/end timestamps per product in the CMS.
  • Product thumbnails on PLPs/PDPs — if a product has been tagged in a short, a small "See it in action" carousel could show on the product page. This creates a nice loop between content and commerce.
  • "Shop the look" as a full outfit — for videos where someone wears multiple items, it could be valuable to present them together as a styled set rather than individual product cards.

We'd love your input on the best way to structure this. The CMS schema in PLAN.md has a suggested approach with per-product timestamps, but it's flexible.

UGC uploads phase 2

Eventually we'd like customers to upload their own clips. The rough flow:

  1. User logs in (Centra customer account).
  2. Upload form: video file, title, optional caption, tag selection, optional product tagging.
  3. Video goes to the hosting service (likely Mux via a direct upload URL — no server bottleneck).
  4. A CMS entry is created with status: pending.
  5. The content team reviews and approves/rejects in Sanity Studio.
  6. Approved clips go live in the feed.

This doesn't need to be in v1 but it's worth keeping in mind architecture-wise — especially around video hosting and the CMS schema.

Technical considerations

Video hosting

The prototype uses local mp4 files. For production, something like Mux would be a good fit — it has a Sanity plugin, does adaptive bitrate (HLS), auto-generates thumbnails, and gives us engagement analytics per video. More details in PLAN.md.

Feed data

The first batch of videos should be server-rendered. After that, infinite scroll loads more via a paginated API (cursor-based, ordered by publish date / priority). The CMS provides all metadata — title, creator, products, tags, publish date.

Performance

  • Only the current video and the next one should be preloaded. Everything else stays at preload="metadata" until it's close to being viewed.
  • On mobile, consider capping resolution at 720p to save bandwidth.
  • Thumbnails on the right side can use static frames (canvas snapshots) rather than live video elements — that's what the prototype does to avoid flicker and reduce memory use.

Deep linking

Each short should have its own URL (/shorts/[slug]) so it can be shared. That page should include proper OG tags (video thumbnail, title) so shared links render a nice preview on social platforms.

Animation reference values

These are the easing curves and timing values used in the prototype. They're a starting point — feel free to adjust, but they're tuned to feel premium and intentional.

Easing curves

NameValueUsed for
expo-outcubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1)Text entrances, thumbnails, general "settling into place"

smooth-decelcubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94)Layout shifts, spacing transitions
ease-in-outcubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1)Height/grid expand (product bubble container)

The prototype in this folder covers the core UX and interaction patterns for both desktop and mobile. It's meant as a starting point — not everything is pixel-final, and there's room to adjust as we build it out together.

Desktop layout

Video player

  • The video sits centered in the content area, below the standard site header/navigation.
  • Video card size: 660 × 825px, border-radius 12px.
  • Background behind the video: #1D1D1D (dark, full-width content area).
  • A subtle bottom gradient overlays the lower portion of the video to make the progress bar readable.

Scroll behavior

  • Vertical scroll with snap-to-center — each scroll gesture locks to the next or previous video.
  • Mouse wheel, keyboard (arrow keys), and click navigation all work.
  • When a clip finishes playing, it automatically advances to the next one.
  • The feed is infinite — when the user reaches the end of the available clips, we shuffle and append more. No clip should appear twice in a row.

Left side — info panel

  • Vertically centered to the left of the video.
  • Shows: title (24px, semibold) and timestamp (16px, light, e.g. "10 min siden").
  • When a video has tagged products, a product thumbnail bubble appears below the title. The bubble has a dark container (135 × 138px, radius 23px, #404040 background) with the product image inside (radius 20px). Some products have a white background instead — this should come from CMS data.
  • Below the bubble: a label like "Used: Crispi Summit GTX" and a "Shop here" button (#AFCC95 pill).
  • Videos without products don't show the bubble or button — just title and timestamp.

Right side — thumbnail navigation

  • Three video thumbnails stacked vertically, showing previous/current/next clips.
  • The active (center) thumbnail is slightly larger than the others — there's a smooth scale transition when scrolling between videos. The inactive ones sit at roughly 82% scale, the active at 100%.
  • Up/down chevron buttons above and below the thumbnails.
  • Clicking a thumbnail jumps to that video.

Progress bar

  • Sits at the bottom of the video card.
  • Dark track (rgba(29, 27, 25, 0.9)), green fill (#7EB896), both rounded.
  • Clickable — the user can click anywhere on the bar to skip to that point in the video.
  • Hit target is larger than the visual bar (20px tall, bar itself is 4px) for easy clicking.

Shop the Look drawer (desktop)

  • Clicking "Shop here" opens a white drawer sliding in from the right (420px wide).
  • Shows all tagged products for that video: image, brand, name, price, and an "Add to cart" button per product.
  • The video pauses while the drawer is open.
  • Closes via the X button, clicking the overlay behind it, or scrolling to a different video.

Mute toggle

  • Bottom-right of the content area.
  • Videos start muted. Click or press M to toggle.

Mobile layout

On mobile the experience shifts to a full-bleed TikTok-style layout:

  • The standard site header is replaced by a compact mobile header: hamburger menu | logo (108px) | cart icon. Below that, a search field.
  • The video fills the entire remaining viewport — no border radius, no side panels.
  • The thumbnail sidebar is hidden. Navigation is swipe-only (vertical scroll snap).
  • Title, timestamp, and a "Se produktene" button overlay the bottom of the video, over a darkened gradient.
  • The product bubble is not shown on mobile (too much visual noise at this size). Instead, the CTA opens a dark drawer from the right (240px, #2a2a2a) with product cards — image, brand, name, price, and add-to-cart.
  • The video pauses while the product drawer is open.
  • The progress bar and mute toggle are repositioned for thumb reach.
Note: The prototype includes a Desktop/Mobile toggle in the top-right corner so you can preview both layouts in one page.

Product tagging not final

This is an area we'd like to explore further — the visual design isn't fully locked down yet, but the concept is important to get into the build early.

The basic idea: each video can have one or more products associated with it. These come from the product catalog (Centra) and are linked in the CMS (Sanity).

Some directions we're thinking about:

  • Timed product tags — a product pill could appear on the video at a specific timestamp (e.g. when the creator holds up a jacket at second 8, the tag pops up and disappears at second 14). This would need start/end timestamps per product in the CMS.
  • Product thumbnails on PLPs/PDPs — if a product has been tagged in a short, a small "See it in action" carousel could show on the product page. This creates a nice loop between content and commerce.
  • "Shop the look" as a full outfit — for videos where someone wears multiple items, it could be valuable to present them together as a styled set rather than individual product cards.

We'd love your input on the best way to structure this. The CMS schema in PLAN.md has a suggested approach with per-product timestamps, but it's flexible.

UGC uploads phase 2

Eventually we'd like customers to upload their own clips. The rough flow:

  1. User logs in (Centra customer account).
  2. Upload form: video file, title, optional caption, tag selection, optional product tagging.
  3. Video goes to the hosting service (likely Mux via a direct upload URL — no server bottleneck).
  4. A CMS entry is created with status: pending.
  5. The content team reviews and approves/rejects in Sanity Studio.
  6. Approved clips go live in the feed.

This doesn't need to be in v1 but it's worth keeping in mind architecture-wise — especially around video hosting and the CMS schema.

Technical considerations

Video hosting

The prototype uses local mp4 files. For production, something like Mux would be a good fit — it has a Sanity plugin, does adaptive bitrate (HLS), auto-generates thumbnails, and gives us engagement analytics per video. More details in PLAN.md.

Feed data

The first batch of videos should be server-rendered. After that, infinite scroll loads more via a paginated API (cursor-based, ordered by publish date / priority). The CMS provides all metadata — title, creator, products, tags, publish date.

Performance

  • Only the current video and the next one should be preloaded. Everything else stays at preload="metadata" until it's close to being viewed.
  • On mobile, consider capping resolution at 720p to save bandwidth.
  • Thumbnails on the right side can use static frames (canvas snapshots) rather than live video elements — that's what the prototype does to avoid flicker and reduce memory use.

Deep linking

Each short should have its own URL (/shorts/[slug]) so it can be shared. That page should include proper OG tags (video thumbnail, title) so shared links render a nice preview on social platforms.

Animation reference values

These are the easing curves and timing values used in the prototype. They're a starting point — feel free to adjust, but they're tuned to feel premium and intentional.

Easing curves

NameValueUsed for
expo-outcubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1)Text entrances, thumbnails, general "settling into place"
overshootcubic-bezier(0.175, 0.885, 0.32, 1.275)CTA button, small UI elements
overshoot-strongcubic-bezier(0.33, 1.42, 0.05, 0.96)Product bubble pop-in (hero moment)
smooth-decelcubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94)Layout shifts, spacing transitions
ease-in-outcubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1)Height/grid expand (product bubble container)

Timing

ElementDurationDelay
Title slide-in550ms0ms
Timestamp slide-in450ms60ms
Product bubble pop600ms140ms
Product label slide400ms400ms
CTA button enter450ms520ms
Bubble container expand650ms—
Thumbnail scale (active)550ms—
Thumbnail scale (inactive)500ms—
Drawer slide-in (desktop)400ms—
Drawer slide-in (mobile)500ms—

Colors

ElementValue
Content area background#1D1D1D
CTA button#AFCC95
Progress bar fill#7EB896
Progress bar trackrgba(29, 27, 25, 0.9)
Product bubble bg#404040 (or #FFFFFF per product)
Mobile drawer bg#2A2A2A
Widforss green#154A29

Prototype and production plan are in the same folder. The prototype runs as a static HTML file — open index.html or serve it locally.

Widforss Shorts

Vertical video feed for widforss.se, v1, June 2025
Prototype: index.html in this folder · Production plan: PLAN.md

Background

We've been thinking about how to bring community content closer to the shopping experience on widforss.se. The idea here is a short-form vertical video feed, along the lines of what TikTok and Reels do, but living on our own site and connected to our product catalog.

The goal is to strengthen social proof and eventually let customers share their own outdoor content, tied directly to products. We've put together a prototype that shows the core interactions for desktop and mobile. It's not pixel-final everywhere, and there are open questions we'd love your thoughts on as we get into the details together.

Desktop layout

Video player

In the prototype, the video card sits centered in a dark content area below the site header. We've been working with a size of 660 x 825px and a 12px border-radius, but happy to discuss if a different proportion works better in the real layout. The dark background is #1D1D1D. There's a subtle gradient at the bottom of the video so the progress bar stays readable.

Scrolling and navigation

The feed scrolls vertically with snap behavior, so each scroll gesture lands on the next (or previous) video. Mouse wheel, keyboard arrows, and clicking the thumbnail navigation on the right all work in the prototype. When a clip finishes playing, it could automatically advance to the next one. The feed loops infinitely by reshuffling clips when you reach the end, making sure the same clip never plays back to back.

Left side info

To the left of the video there's a small info panel, vertically centered. It shows the video title and a relative timestamp ("10 min siden"). When a video has tagged products, a product thumbnail bubble appears below the text, with a label like "Used: Crispi Summit GTX" and a "Shop here" button. Videos without tagged products just show the title and time.

The product bubble is a rounded container (roughly 135 x 138px, #404040 background) with the product image inside. For some products (like lighter-colored items) a white background might work better. This could be controlled per product in the CMS.

Right side thumbnails

On the right side of the video there are three small video thumbnails stacked vertically, representing the previous, current, and next clips. The active one is slightly larger than the other two (scale difference of about 0.82 vs 1.0), and they transition smoothly when you scroll between videos. Clicking a thumbnail could jump to that video. There are also small up/down arrows above and below.

Progress bar

At the bottom of the video card. Dark track, green fill (#7EB896), both rounded. It's clickable so you can skip to any point in the video. The visual bar is 4px but the clickable area is taller (around 20px) for easy interaction.

Product drawer

When you click "Shop here", a drawer slides in from the right showing all the tagged products for that video. Each product has an image, brand, name, price, and an add-to-cart button. The video pauses while the drawer is open. It closes by clicking X, clicking the overlay, or just scrolling to a different video.

Mute

Videos start muted. There's a toggle in the bottom right corner (also works with the M key).

Mobile layout

On mobile the layout changes quite a bit. The idea is something closer to a TikTok-style full-screen experience:

  • The desktop header is replaced by a compact mobile header (hamburger | small logo | cart) with a search field below it.
  • The video fills the remaining viewport. No border radius, no side panels.
  • Navigation is swipe only (the thumbnail sidebar is hidden).
  • Title, timestamp, and a "Se produktene" button are overlaid on the bottom of the video, over a darkened gradient for contrast.
  • Instead of the product bubble, tapping "Se produktene" opens a dark drawer from the right (around 240px wide, #2a2a2a) with compact product cards. The video pauses while it's open.
  • Progress bar and mute are repositioned for thumb reach.
Tip: The prototype has a Desktop/Mobile toggle in the top right corner so you can switch between both views.

Product tagging exploring

This is something we're still figuring out visually, but the concept feels important to include early. Each video can have one or more products associated with it, linked from the product catalog (Centra) via the CMS (Sanity).

A few directions we've been discussing:

  • Timed tags on the video. For example, when someone in the video holds up a jacket at a specific moment, a small product pill could pop up on the video for a few seconds. This would need start/end timestamps per product in the CMS. Would be great to hear if there's a clean way to model this.
  • Shorts on product pages. If a product has been featured in a short, we could show a small "See it in action" section on the PDP. Could be a nice way to connect content and commerce both ways.
  • Full outfit view. For videos where someone is wearing several items, it might make sense to present them as a styled look rather than individual cards. Not sure yet how this would look exactly.

Open to suggestions on how to approach this. PLAN.md has a draft CMS schema with per-product timestamps, but nothing is set in stone.

UGC uploads phase 2

Down the line, it would be great if customers could upload their own clips. We're imagining something like:

  1. User logs in with their customer account.
  2. Simple upload form: pick a video, add a title, maybe select some tags or link products.
  3. The video gets uploaded directly to the hosting service (we've been looking at Mux for this, since it has a Sanity plugin and handles large files well).
  4. A draft entry appears in the CMS for the content team to review.
  5. Once approved, the clip goes live in the feed.

This is definitely a later phase, but worth keeping in mind when thinking about the CMS schema and video hosting setup.

Technical notes

Video hosting

The prototype just uses local mp4 files. For production, Mux seems like a strong option. It has a first-party Sanity plugin, supports adaptive streaming (HLS), generates thumbnails automatically, and includes engagement analytics. There's more detail on this in PLAN.md, but happy to discuss alternatives.

Feed data

We're thinking the first batch of videos could be server-rendered, and then infinite scroll loads more via a paginated API. Cursor-based pagination probably makes sense, ordered by publish date or some priority field. All the metadata (title, creator, products, tags) would come from Sanity.

Performance thoughts

  • Probably a good idea to only preload the current and next video. Everything else can stay at preload="metadata" until it's about to scroll into view.
  • On mobile, capping at 720p could help with bandwidth.
  • For the desktop thumbnails on the right, we ended up using canvas snapshots of single video frames rather than live video elements. It avoids flicker when switching and keeps memory usage low. Could be worth doing the same in production.

Deep linking

It would be nice if each short had its own URL (something like /shorts/[slug]) so people can share them. With proper OG tags, shared links would show a video thumbnail and title on social platforms, which could help drive traffic back to the site.

Animation reference

These are the easing curves and timing values from the prototype, mostly for reference. They're tuned to feel smooth and considered, but not precious about the exact numbers if you find something that works better.

Easing curves

NameValueUsed for
expo-outcubic-bezier(0.19, 1, 0.22, 1)Text entrances, thumbnails, general settling
overshootcubic-bezier(0.175, 0.885, 0.32, 1.275)CTA button, small UI elements
overshoot-strongcubic-bezier(0.33, 1.42, 0.05, 0.96)Product bubble entrance
smooth-decelcubic-bezier(0.25, 0.46, 0.45, 0.94)Layout shifts, spacing changes
ease-in-outcubic-bezier(0.65, 0, 0.35, 1)Container height transitions

Timing

ElementDurationDelay
Title550ms0ms
Timestamp450ms60ms
Product bubble600ms140ms
Product label400ms400ms
CTA button450ms520ms
Bubble container expand650ms
Thumbnail scale (up)550ms
Thumbnail scale (down)500ms
Desktop drawer400ms
Mobile drawer500ms

Colors

ElementValue
Content area background#1D1D1D
CTA button#AFCC95
Progress bar fill#7EB896
Progress bar trackrgba(29, 27, 25, 0.9)
Product bubble bg#404040 (or #FFFFFF per product)
Mobile drawer bg#2A2A2A
Widforss green#154A29

! gh auth login

We're thinking the first batch of videos could be server-rendered, and then infinite scroll loads more via a paginated API. Probably a good idea to only preload the current and next video. Everything else can stay at preload="metadata" until it's about to scroll into view.
On mobile, capping at 720p could help with bandwidth.
